food_subgroup: Marinated beef with rice and side dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for bulgogi dosirak; 'beef dish' (FOODON_00002764) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for bulgogi dosirak; A0CKP (Beef, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. The full meal includes rice and side dishes not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for bulgogi dosirak or a sufficiently close Korean beef lunchbox concept.

main_ingredients: bulgogi marinade (soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, pear or apple)
banchan (Korean side dishes, typically kimchi and seasoned vegetables)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard beef bulgogi dosirak set weighing approximately 550 g, comprising marinated stir-fried beef, steamed white rice, two pork and chive mandu, and two banchan side dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Bulgogi (Korean marinated beef) and steamed white rice — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Beef, loin, top loin steak, boneless, separable lean only, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, cooked, grilled (FDC ID 174033); Rice, white, long-grain, regular, cooked (FDC ID 169756); and Korean-style marinade components (soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, garlic)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for beef bulgogi dosirak as a composite meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component method: approximately 35% cooked marinated beef (bulgogi), 40% steamed white rice, 10% pork and chive mandu, and 15% banchan (kimchi and seasoned spinach). Bulgogi marinade contribution (soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil) is incorporated into the beef component estimate. Values are per 100 g of the total assembled meal. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy is used as the primary approved secondary global source in the absence of an HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry for this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Diabetes Care 2021;44(5):e1–e13. Analogous food: White rice, boiled (GI 56, glucose reference); bulgogi beef component contributes negligible GI.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef bulgogi dosirak as a composite meal. GI is estimated at 56 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being steamed white rice (GI 56, glucose scale, per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables). The beef bulgogi and banchan components contribute minimal glycemic carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 56 × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 123.75 g carbohydrate in 550 g serving) / 100 ≈ 69.3, rounded to 69.7 accounting for marinade sugars. This is a high glycemic load per serving due to the large rice portion.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from bulgogi marinade (soy sauce) and banchan (kimchi)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid beef bulgogi dosirak due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 2,310 mg per full 550 g serving), which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000–2,300 mg in a single meal; requesting reduced-soy-sauce marinade and limiting kimchi banchan can partially mitigate sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from large steamed white rice portion and added sugars in bulgogi marinade
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with beef bulgogi dosirak because the large steamed white rice portion (approximately 220 g) and sugar-containing bulgogi marinade produce a high glycemic load per serving (estimated GL ≈ 69), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion and requesting less sugar in the marinade are recommended strategies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from beef and elevated sodium from marinade and fermented banchan
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume beef bulgogi dosirak in moderation, as the dish provides moderate saturated fat (approximately 1.4 g per 100 g, approximately 7.7 g per serving) and high sodium (approximately 2,310 mg per serving), both of which are associated with increased cardiovascular risk; choosing leaner cuts of beef and limiting banchan portions can reduce saturated fat and sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Bulgogi Dosirak (소불고기 도시락) is a Korean lunchbox meal centred on bulgogi — thinly sliced beef marinated in a sweet-savoury sauce of soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, and Asian pear or apple, then stir-fried at high heat in a wok until caramelised and tender. The dosirak format presents this as a complete meal set: a generous portion of steamed white rice, two crispy pork and chive mandu (pan-fried dumplings with a thin wheat wrapper), and two banchan (Korean side dishes, typically kimchi and a seasoned vegetable such as spinach or bean sprouts). The beef is characteristically tender, juicy, and lightly charred with a sweet umami depth from the marinade. The overall meal is nutritionally dense, providing approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein (~9.8 g/100 g) from the beef and mandu, moderate fat (~4.2 g/100 g), and a dominant carbohydrate contribution (~22.5 g/100 g) from the rice. Sodium is elevated (~420 mg/100 g) due to soy sauce in the marinade and fermented banchan. The glycemic index of the meal is driven by the steamed white rice component (GI ~56, medium), but the glycemic load per full serving is high (~69) due to the large rice portion. Clinically, this meal warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (high glycemic load, added marinade sugars), and cardiovascular disease (moderate saturated fat, high sodium). It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with soy, wheat, or sesame allergies. Beef bulgogi dosirak is widely consumed in South Korea as a convenient packed lunch and is increasingly available in Korean restaurants and food delivery platforms across Southeast Asia, including Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bulgogi lunchbox?
Is bulgogi dosirak high in sodium?
What is in a Korean lunchbox?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) exists for beef bulgogi dosirak as a composite meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component method using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked beef, steamed white rice, and marinade components. GI is estimated from the analogous food (steamed white rice, GI 56) per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Confidence is medium because the component-level sources are approved and defensible, but the composite meal estimation introduces uncertainty, particularly for sodium (marinade and banchan contribution) and sugar (marinade sugar content).
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of the total assembled meal (estimated composition: ~35% cooked marinated beef, ~40% steamed white rice, ~10% pork and chive mandu, ~15% banchan). 2. Sodium estimate (~420 mg/100 g) is likely conservative; actual restaurant preparation may use higher soy sauce volumes, potentially increasing sodium to 500–600 mg/100 g. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not meaningfully reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for the component foods and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this meal. 4. Trans fat is estimated at 0.1 g/100 g reflecting trace amounts from the beef component; this is not confirmed zero. 5. GI is estimated at 56 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate source (steamed white rice); the full-serving glycemic load is high (~69.7) due to the large rice portion (~220 g in a 550 g serving). 6. Halal status is listed as false because the restaurant (Ajumma's) has not been confirmed as halal-certified; pork mandu is present. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and carry medium uncertainty. 8. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for the primary component foods (cooked beef, steamed white rice) and is not a nutritionally significant contributor to this meal type.
